---
title: Organizing website content
---

A Quarto website has two related structures:

- The file structure, which determines where source documents and resources
  live in the repository.
- The navigation structure, which determines how readers move through the
  rendered website.

These structures often resemble each other, but they are not the same. Moving
a `.qmd` file into a folder does not automatically place it in the sidebar,
and changing sidebar order does not require moving the source file.

## Find the website configuration

Website navigation is usually defined in `_quarto.yml`, or in a metadata file
loaded by `_quarto.yml`. Look for a `website:` section containing `navbar:`
and/or `sidebar:`.

```yaml
website:
  title: "Course notes"
  sidebar:
    contents:
      - index.qmd
      - lessons/introduction.qmd
      - lessons/enzymes.qmd
```

The list under `contents` controls the displayed order. A page can exist and
render successfully without appearing in navigation, so always check the
rendered site after adding material.

## Choose a reader-facing structure

Organize navigation around what readers need to find, rather than around every
internal repository directory. Common top-level areas include:

- Course overview or home page.
- Weekly lessons or exercises.
- Practical guides and setup material.
- Datasets, downloads, or reference material.

A small website can use one sidebar with sections:

```yaml
website:
  sidebar:
    style: "docked"
    contents:
      - href: index.qmd
        text: Home
      - section: "Exercises"
        contents:
          - exercises/week-01.qmd
          - exercises/week-02.qmd
      - section: "Resources"
        contents:
          - resources/datasets.qmd
          - resources/software.qmd
```

Use a landing page such as `exercises/index.qmd` when a section benefits from
an introduction, instructions, or a table of contents of its own.

## Navbar and separate sidebars

For a larger site, the navbar can identify major areas while sidebars provide
the detailed sequence within each area. For example, a site may give
exercises, software guides, and videos their own navbar destinations and
their own sidebar groups:

```yaml
website:
  navbar:
    left:
      - href: index.qmd
        text: Home
      - href: exercises/index.qmd
        text: Exercises
      - href: guides/index.qmd
        text: Guides

  sidebar:
    - id: exercises-sidebar
      contents:
        - section: "Exercises"
          contents:
            - exercises/week-01.qmd
            - exercises/week-02.qmd
    - id: guides-sidebar
      contents:
        - section: "Guides"
          contents:
            - guides/installation.qmd
            - guides/using-software.qmd
```

This pattern is useful when each area contains many pages. Keep the navbar
short: it should expose major destinations, not duplicate every sidebar item.

## Explicit order or automatic inclusion

An explicit list makes teaching sequence and omissions easy to review:

```yaml
contents:
  - lessons/week-01.qmd
  - lessons/week-02.qmd
  - lessons/revision.qmd
```

A wildcard is convenient for a regular collection of pages:

```yaml
contents: lessons/*.qmd
```

Use a wildcard only where the filenames give a reliable order and every
matching document belongs in navigation. Prefer an explicit list when:

- Lessons must appear in a deliberate teaching sequence.
- Draft or helper `.qmd` files are stored in the same folder.
- Navigation text or grouping needs individual control.

Some projects also build listing pages from a directory. A listing can give
readers an overview of automatically discovered lessons, while the sidebar
still controls how they browse the site.

## Add a new page

When adding a lesson or guide:

1. Place the new `.qmd` source document in the appropriate content folder.
2. Add referenced figures, datasets, or downloads at paths that make sense
   alongside the existing resources.
3. Add the new document to the appropriate sidebar section, unless a reviewed
   wildcard or listing intentionally handles it.
4. Add or update a navbar destination only if a new major site area is being
   introduced.
5. Preview the site and follow the navigation link to the new page.

For a site with student and instructor profiles, keep shared navigation in
the base configuration where possible, and preview both profiles if the new
page contains solution-controlled content.

## Reorganize existing material

Reorganizing navigation without moving files is usually the lowest-risk
change: change section headings or item order in the sidebar and render the
site.

If source files also need to move or be renamed:

1. Move the `.qmd` source document and any resources that should move with it.
2. Update sidebar and navbar paths in the Quarto configuration.
3. Update links, image paths, includes, download-button paths, and Colab or
   external links that point at the old location.
4. Check any `project.render`, `resources`, `resource-path`, listing, or
   workflow configuration that selects files by path.
5. Render every published profile and output format affected by the move.
6. Inspect `git diff` to confirm that generated output is not replacing
   intended source changes.

::: {.callout-warning title="Profiles should share information architecture"}
Student and instructor output may differ in answer visibility, but readers
should normally find the same lesson at the same conceptual location. Avoid
maintaining unrelated sidebar organizations for the two profiles unless
there is a specific publication requirement.
:::

## Existing course patterns

- `imol` uses a navbar entry for its Python introduction and an explicitly
  ordered sidebar for the individual lessons.
- `structure_biology` divides a larger site into navbar areas with dedicated
  sidebar groups for exercises, guides, setup material, and videos.
- `fysisk_biokemi` uses wildcard sidebar contents for regular lesson and quiz
  collections, together with a listing-based landing page.

Choose the simplest pattern that makes the learner's path through the
material obvious, then keep the structure consistent as the course grows.
